planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moist, deep, fertile to average loamy or sandy soil in partial shade to full sun; western hemlock grows naturally in the moist Pacific coastal and Cascade forests where high humidity and consistent soil moisture are available year-round. Does not tolerate drought or heat.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in moist, slightly acidic, deep, fertile to average loamy or sandy soil in partial shade to full sun; western hemlock is the state tree of Washington and one of the dominant trees of the moist Pacific Northwest forests from northern California to Alaska — often growing alongside Sitka spruce, Douglas-fir, and Pacific red cedar in the classic coastal rainforest community. The nodding leading shoot and graceful, pendulous branch tips distinguish it from all other conifers. The small, oval cones (less than an inch long) are among the smallest of any large conifer. Western hemlock is remarkable for its shade tolerance — young seedlings establish readily in the deep shade of old-growth forest and slowly grow up to eventually overtop their nurse trees. The inner bark was used by Pacific Northwest indigenous peoples as an emergency food source. The hemlock bark is high in tannin and was an important tanning material in the Pacific Northwest leather industry.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or loamy, average to lean soil in full sun; Cedar of Lebanon grows naturally on well-drained limestone slopes of the mountains of Lebanon, Turkey, and Syria. It tolerates drought and lean soils but requires excellent drainage — wet, heavy soils cause rapid decline.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or loamy, lean to average soil in full sun; Cedar of Lebanon is the most historically and culturally significant conifer in the world — the tree of the ancient Middle East, used to build King Solomon's Temple, Noah's Ark in the Midrash, and the fleets of the Phoenician traders. A young tree is non-descript, but old trees (100+ years) develop the flat, table-top, broadly spreading crown with massive horizontal limb tiers that is instantly recognizable — the form that appears on the flag of Lebanon. The cedar forests of Lebanon were already depleted by ancient times; only a few groves of ancient trees survive today on the slopes of the Cedars of God reserve. Plant in a very large, permanent space — old trees develop a majestic, wide-spreading crown wider than they are tall. More drought and alkaline-soil tolerant than deodar cedar. Cold-hardy to zone 5.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, moist, fertile to average, loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; hinoki cypress prefers moist, well-drained, slightly acidic conditions and does not tolerate drought, waterlogged soil, or alkaline conditions. Cultivated varieties range from dwarf groundcovers to large trees.

directions: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, moist, fertile to average, loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; hinoki cypress is a sacred conifer of Japan, used to build Shinto shrines and the most important temples in Kyoto and Nara — the fragrant, pale-blond wood is the most valued timber species in Japan. An enormous species in the wild (reaching 100+ feet in the mountains of Japan), it is the parent of literally hundreds of cultivated varieties ranging from tiny, 2-inch bun-shaped dwarfs to golden, pendulous, or billowing forms of every size and habit. The distinctive flattened frond-like foliage sprays with bright white markings on the undersides are characteristic. Cultivars such as 'Nana Gracilis', 'Filicoides', 'Crippsii', and 'Gracilis' are among the most popular ornamental conifers in the world. The variable spacing and height reflects the wide range of cultivated forms available.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown trees in spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moist, fertile to average, deep loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; giant arborvitae is more adaptable to moisture and shade than eastern arborvitae (T. occidentalis). It grows in very moist conditions and tolerates temporary flooding.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown trees in spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moist, fertile to average, deep loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; giant arborvitae (western red cedar) is among the most significant trees in the cultures of the Pacific Northwest indigenous peoples — the canoe tree, the totem pole tree, the longhouse tree, whose rot-resistant red wood served as the primary building material across the coastal Northwest. Despite the common name 'western red cedar,' it is not a true cedar but a member of the Cupressaceae. It grows to over 200 feet tall with a massively buttressed base in the moist coastal rainforests from northern California to Alaska. The foliage has a distinctive pineapple-like fragrance when crushed. Widely planted as a fast-growing hedge and privacy screen in the Pacific Northwest. The cultivar 'Green Giant' (a Thuja plicata hybrid) is the most widely planted privacy screen tree in the eastern US.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown plants in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or loamy, dry to average soil in full sun; Chinese juniper is very drought tolerant and adapts to poor, dry, alkaline soils — making it one of the most widely used landscape junipers in the US. Avoid wet, poorly drained conditions.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown plants in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or loamy, dry to average soil in full sun; Chinese juniper is the most important juniper species in horticulture — the parent of an enormous number of hybrid and cultivated varieties that dominate commercial landscape planting across North America. Cultivars range from flat-spreading groundcovers (Pfitzer junipers, Juniperus x pfitzeriana, are crosses with J. sabina) to columnar accent trees and compact mound forms. The species itself varies from a large, upright tree form to spreading shrub forms. The iconic 'Blue Point' (pyramidal), 'Torulosa' (irregular, Hollywood juniper), 'Kaizuka' (spreading-irregular), 'Blue Vase', 'Old Gold', and 'Sea Green' are all widely planted cultivars or hybrids involving this species. Exceptionally tolerant of dry, poor, alkaline soils, heat, and cold — the workhorse conifer of American commercial landscaping.
